The first idea they tell us is that a person can have a true thought even when the reason they use is not a good one. In other words, the thought can be the same as what is really true, and the reason that made the person have that thought is a bad one.

The second idea they add is that because the reason is bad, the person does not really know the thing, even though the thought is true. So they say that the true thought does not count as a real know, and that this is not right.

The third idea they think follows is that to know something you must have a true thought and also a good reason for that thought. They think that the good reason makes the true thought a thing that you can use again and again, and that without it the true thought is just a true thought.

When we look at the thinking, the first part that a true thought can be true is a strong point. The second part that a bad reason makes the true thought a thing you can use again and again is also true, because we often have a true thought that came from a good reason. The part that says a true thought is the same as a good reason is a step that many people agree with, but it is not all the way true. english_query: counterexample to: A theodicy T successfully reconciles the existence of a benevolent omnipotent deity with the existence of evil if and only if (i) T provides a morally sufficient reason for the deity's permission of evil, (ii) the reason cited in T is consistent with the deity's omnipotence and benevolence, and (iii) T accounts for the quantity and distribution of evil observed in the world.

english_query: counterexample to: An omission O by a person P is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) P had a duty to act, (ii) H would not have occurred had P fulfilled that duty, and (iii) P's failure to act was the sole cause of H.
ugf_response:
The first idea is that there are some things a person can have to give up when they do this, no matter how big the reason behind it is. The person says that even if doing that thing would make many other people happy, it is still not a good thing to do. The second idea is that the idea of never doing those things is a big idea that works in every case. The third idea is that because of that idea, we must always keep away from any other thing we do.

The first idea is true in many times. We can see many times that when one person hurts another, the other person feels pain and may not feel safe. The second idea is also true in the sense that most people do not want to be hurt, and many people want to be safe. The third idea is the thought that we should follow the idea because it is a big idea that says do not do that.

The thinking that follows from the second and third ideas is that the best thing to do is to always stay away from any thing we do that would cause hurt to another person, even if it would help many other people. The thinking that follows is that we must always look for another thing we do that does not cause hurt. That thinking is a good way to act because it keeps people safe and makes the world a place where people can live together without fear.

But the thinking

english_query: counterexample to: A region R is a space if and only if (i) R is bounded — that is, R has limits or borders that distinguish it from what lies outside, (ii) R is capable of containing material objects, and (iii) the positions of objects within R can be specified by a finite set of numerical coordinates.
ugf_response: The five ways show that we change from being wrong because they hide our true beliefs.

english_query: counterexample to: A person A has an obligation to perform action X if and only if (i) A has voluntarily undertaken to perform X, (ii) another person B has a legitimate expectation that A will perform X, and (iii) failure to perform X would cause harm to B.
ugf_response: The idea is true because people often keep what they have instead of getting more, showing that feeling about taking chances can make choices hard.

english_query: counterexample to: A word W has meaning if and only if (i) W is associated with a mental image in the mind of the speaker, (ii) the speaker can use W to communicate with others, and (iii) W occurs in a natural language that is spoken by a community.
ugf_response:
The person is talking about why the way a person acts or the way they are inside does not tell us if what they say is true. The first idea they give is that the way a person is inside the way they act and the things they do in their life, or the way they are not inside does not change whether their words are the same as the world. The second idea they give is that we can look at a thing they say and ask if it is the same as what we know is true. If the thing is the same as the world, then it can be true. If it is not the same as the world, then it is not true. This part of the case rests on the idea that the truth of a thing does not rest on who says it.

From these ideas they think that we should not let the way a person is inside decide if the thing they say is true. They think that if we accept the thing they say just because the person is good or bad, we are doing something wrong. They also think that we should keep the truth of a thing away from the person who says it. In short, they think that the truth of a thing stands on its own, and we must look at the thing itself, not the person, to see if it is true.

The thinking in the case is mostly good. It is good because it keeps the truth away

english_query: counterexample to: A political entity E is sovereign if and only if (i) E exercises supreme authority within a defined territory — no higher authority overrides its decisions, (ii) E controls the use of force within that territory, and (iii) E is recognised as sovereign by other sovereign entities.
ugf_response:
The person is making a case about why the ways we must act should be the same for every person. First, the person says that there are ways of acting that are good and ways of acting that are bad. Second, the person says that these ways of acting should not change from one person to another. Third, the person says that if we want to know if a way of acting is good, we must look at what would happen if every person did that same thing all the time.

The person thinks that if we say a way of acting is good, we must be able to say that it is good for everyone at all times. They think that if we say a way of acting is good, we must be able to say it is good for every person in that same way. They think that if we say a way of acting is bad, it must be bad for everyone.

We must look at if this way of thinking is good. The first thing to look at is if the idea is true. It is true that some ways of acting make things good for people, but it is hard to make things the very same for every person without making things go wrong. For one thing, a person might think it is good to tell a lie to get what they want, but that would be a bad thing if everyone told lies. The second thing to look at is if the idea is true.

english_query: counterexample to: A property is definable in a formal language if and only if there exists a formula in that language that is satisfied by exactly those objects possessing the property, the formula is finitely long, and the property is extensional.
ugf_response:
The first idea is that there is a space between a reason that is very good and a reason that is not good at all. The person says that some ways of thinking lead us to what is true more often than other ways. They say that some ways of thinking make it very possible that our thought is right, while other ways make it only a little bit possible.

The second idea is what follows from this. If we can see the space between these two ways of thinking, then we can tell if a way of thinking is good or bad. If we use the very good ways, we will find the truth more often. If we use the ways that are not good, we will find things that are not true. This means we must look at how much power a reason has before we say a thought is right.

The third idea is whether this way of thinking is good. To know if this is good, we must see if the way they tell us to find the truth is itself good. If they can show us how to tell the way things are not the same, then their way of thinking is good. However, if they do not tell us how to do this, then their way of thinking is not good. We must see if their way of telling us to find the truth is itself a good way to find the truth.

english_query: counterexample to: A person possesses intellectual humility if and only if (i) the person has an accurate assessment of the limits of the person's own knowledge and cognitive abilities, (ii) the person does not claim greater epistemic authority than is warranted by the person's actual competence, and (iii) the person is disposed to acknowledge the person's own errors and to credit the contributions of others.

The person is talking about a way to show why something must be true. The first idea they give is that a person has a certain way of being. They think that if you look at the person instead of the thing, you will do something that is not right. They think that the truth of a thing stays the same even if a bad person says it.

We must now look at if this way of thinking is good. The way they think is strong in one way. It is true that how a person is and what a person says are two different things. One is about the person and the other is about the world. One is about how a person is. The other is about what is true. However, there is a problem with this way of thinking. Sometimes

english_query: counterexample to: A person has broken a promise if and only if (i) the person uttered words to the effect that they would perform a specified action, (ii) the promisee relied upon the assurance, and (iii) the person failed to perform the action.

The person wants to tell us why some things that people say they will do stay the same and why some things that people say they will do break into many pieces. The first idea is that a thing that people say they will do stays together when the people in it feel that the thing they get is as good as the work they have to do. The second idea is that when people feel that the thing they get is not as good as the work they have to do, they will want to stop the thing they said they would do. The third idea is that when the things each person gets are not as good as the work they have to do, the thing they said they would do will break.

The person thinks that if you want to make sure people keep their word, you must first make sure that the things each person gets are the same as the work they have to do. Then you must also make sure that the people will want to keep their word. This leads to the thought that people will stay together only when the things they get are good enough and when the feeling of trust is high.

When we look at the thinking, we see that it is a good way to think about why some things stay together and why others break. It uses the feeling that people want the same kind of good thing and the fear of hurt to tell why some things stay together. If those two things are true, then the thinking

english_query: counterexample to: An event E constitutes a genuine revolution if and only if (i) E involves the overthrow of an existing political order, (ii) E occurs rapidly relative to the normal pace of political change, and (iii) E results in a new political order that is qualitatively different from its predecessor.
ugf_response: The first idea says that an order is a set of words that tell people what to do. This part can be true because a order is a thing that tells people what to do. The second idea says that just doing the same as the order will make a person do what the order says, even if the person does not think about the other people who are around them. That part can be true. a order that is made by one group can be made by a few who do not see all the ways a thing can be done. It can also be not true, because a person can still do the same as the order but still act in a way that makes another person feel sad or hurt. So the thinking that follows from the first idea is not always a good way to act. It is better to think about the other people, to see if they are hurt or happy, and to use the order in a way that also helps the person who is being looked at. In that sense the thinking that you need both the order and the feeling of the other people is a good way to think.

english_query: counterexample to: An argument for God's existence is compelling if and only if (i) it proceeds from uncontroversial empirical premises, (ii) the inference to a divine cause is the only plausible explanation, and (iii) the conclusion specifies the attributes of the God whose existence is proved.
ugf_response: The most strong answer is the plan that uses three things that use true starting ideas and tells others what to do.

english_query: counterexample to: Two or more agents cooperate if and only if (i) each agent is aware that they are participating in a joint activity, (ii) each agent intentionally contributes to a shared goal, and (iii) the agents have communicated with one another about their respective roles.
ugf_response: Both people end up getting a small number of money, even though they would be better off if they both chose to help.

english_query: counterexample to: A proposition P is a tautology if and only if (i) P is true in all possible circumstances, (ii) P's truth can be established by examining its logical form alone, without knowledge of the truth-values of its components, and (iii) P is expressible using only the standard truth-functional connectives.
ugf_response:
The first idea is that there are two ways to hold a thought about something. One way is to have a thought that is true. The other way is to have a thought that is not true. The person says that a person can have a thought that is true, but they can still do things that are not true. The second idea is that the person who has a true thought for the wrong reasons is not the same as knowing. This person says that if you get to a true thought by a thing that happens by chance or by a bad way of thinking, you do not really know the thing.

The person thinks that because of these ideas, we must say that having a true thought is not the same as knowing. They think that knowing needs a good way of getting to the truth. If the way you got to the truth is bad, then even if you are right, you do not have the kind of truth that people call knowing.

We must look at if this way of thinking is good. The thinking is good because it helps us see how one thing is not the same as another thing. It helps us see that knowing is more than just being right by a thing that happens by chance. If we say that being right by chance is the same as knowing, then we would have to say that a person who wins a game by a thing that happens by chance knows the way to win.

english_query: counterexample to: An event E constitutes a genuine revolution if and only if (i) E involves the overthrow of an existing political order, (ii) E occurs rapidly relative to the normal pace of political change, and (iii) E results in a new political order that is qualitatively different from its predecessor.

english_query: counterexample to: A property is definable in a formal language if and only if there exists a formula in that language that is satisfied by exactly those objects possessing the property, the formula is finitely long, and the property is extensional.

english_query: counterexample to: A set of sentences is consistent if and only if there exists a model that satisfies all members of the set, no sentence in the set is the negation of another, and the set is finite.
ugf_response: When a big group takes over, the shared feeling of being right grows because everyone waits for someone else to go first, which breaks the quiet plan that saves lives.

english_query: counterexample to: A narrative N constitutes a legitimate historical narrative if and only if (i) N recounts events in their correct temporal sequence, (ii) N is grounded in authentic primary sources, and (iii) N provides a coherent account of why events occurred as they did.
ugf_response:
The first idea they say is true is that a person can have a true thought about a thing and get that thought because of a bad cause. They point out that the thought is true, and they give a reason that makes the person think the thought is true.

From that they move to the next idea that because the thought is true, the person now has a kind of knowing. They say that this kind of knowing is what we call knowing, and that a true thought that comes from a bad cause is not the same as knowing. In other words, they think the truth of the thought alone is enough to say the person knows.

Looking at the thinking, the step from the first idea to the second idea makes sense: a good cause helps the person get the true thought, and a bad cause does not. That step is easy to see and can be good for many cases, because the first idea tells us that a true thought is not enough for real knowing. However, the thinking can miss some other points. Sometimes a person can have a true thought because they saw a sign, heard a story, or used a good cause, and that can still be called knowing. Also, a person can still be right about the thought even though the way they got it was not good, because the reason they used was not good. So the step from the first idea to the second idea is not always strong.

english_query: counterexample to: A person P perceives an object O if and only if (i) P has a sensory experience with phenomenal content that represents O, (ii) P can attend to O and discriminate O from its surroundings, and (iii) P can identify O — that is, P can say what O is.
ugf_response:
The first idea is that a person can change the way they are by changing the things they do. This means that the things a person does every day can change the way they feel about the person they are and the way they see that person. The second idea is that if you want to change who you are, you must first change the things you do. The person thinks that if you start doing things that a good person would do, you will slowly become a good person.

We must look at if this way of thinking is good. The first idea is true because people can choose to do new things. We can choose to act in ways that show who we are to others. However, the second idea might not be true. It is possible to do things in a new way but still feel the same inside. It is also possible to do things that look good to others but are bad to others. So, the way of thinking is not always right. It is a good way to think about how to change, but it is not a way that is always true in every case.

english_query: counterexample to: A judgement is synthetic a priori if and only if (i) the predicate is not contained in the concept of the subject, (ii) the judgement is known independently of experience, and (iii) the judgement's truth is grounded in the pure forms of intuition or the categories of the understanding.
ugf_response: The best thing to do is to let the person who made the talk have good reasons to make others believe they did well.

english_query: counterexample to: A translation T of a sentence S from language L1 into language L2 is adequate if and only if (i) T preserves the truth-conditions of S, (ii) T is grammatically well-formed in L2, and (iii) T employs vocabulary in L2 that corresponds to the vocabulary of S in L1.
ugf_response: The teacher uses words that are clear but cannot be true as an easy yes or no, so we should take them as real.

english_query: counterexample to: An act of naming succeeds in assigning a name N to an object O if and only if (i) a speaker utters N with the intention of designating O, (ii) the speaker is in perceptual contact with O at the time of the utterance, and (iii) the speaker has the concept of naming — that is, the speaker understands that the utterance is meant to establish a lasting designation.
ugf_response: The speaker should think that saying the speaker is good can help the speaker make clear choices and understand the papers.

english_query: counterexample to: A belief is dispositional if and only if (i) the subject is disposed to assent to the proposition when the question is suitably raised, (ii) this disposition is grounded in some prior cognitive episode or process, and (iii) the subject would, under normal conditions, affirm the proposition sincerely upon reflection.
ugf_response: The person who talks does not accept the thing that a person who does not trust others has. They also do not accept the thing that a person who lives in a house who has power says. They may add words like You are the one who must do this or You are not the one who must do this. But the person who talks says the thing that a person who is the one who tells others what to do is right.

english_query: counterexample to: A painting represents an object if and only if it visually resembles that object in relevant respects, competent viewers recognise the object in the painting, and the painter intended to depict that object.
ugf_response:
The first idea is that the way a person shows different ways to act can change which way a person is inside. The person says that how you see yourself and how you act in a group is not the same thing. The second idea is that the things you do every day are the things that make you who you are. The third idea is that if you change the things you do, you will change who you are.

From these ideas, the person thinks that a person can become a new kind of person. If a person wants to be a different kind of person, they do not need to wait for a change inside their heart or mind. Instead, they can just start doing different things. By doing these new things, the person will slowly become the new person they want to be.

We must look at if this way of thinking is good. The thinking is good because it gives people power to change. It says that we are not stuck being the same way forever. However, we must ask if doing something is the same as being something. If a person does a kind thing, does it make them a kind person? The thinking is strong because it shows that the things we do and the person we are are tied together. It is not just that we are stuck, but that what we do can change what we are.

english_query: counterexample to: A theodicy T successfully reconciles the existence of a benevolent omnipotent deity with the existence of evil if and only if (i) T provides a morally sufficient reason for the deity's permission of evil, (ii) the reason cited in T is consistent with the deity's omnipotence and benevolence, and (iii) T accounts for the quantity and distribution of evil observed in the world.
ugf_response:
The first idea they say is that a human can have a true thought that comes from a bad reason, such as a lucky guess, a lie, or a lie that happens to fit the situation. In other words, the true thought is there, but the way the thought came about does not line up with how the world works. This is the first idea.

From that first idea they think that the only thing that matters is whether the thought is true. If the reason behind the thought is a bad one, then the thought is not the same as knowing. They also think that a good reason is needed for knowing, so a true thought with a bad reason does not count as knowing. They may add that we should look at the reason behind the thought, not just the truth of the thought itself.

Looking at the first idea, it is true that a true thought can happen even if the reason behind it is not good. For a case, a person may see a broken thing and think it is broken because a cat pushed it, even though the cat was on a bad path. The thought that the cat was on a bad path is not the same as knowing that the cat is on a bad path. So the first idea that true thoughts can happen without a good reason is a true thought, the second idea that the true thought alone is enough for knowing is not true, and the thinking that the

english_query: counterexample to: A person S is entitled to restitution from a person T if and only if (i) T has been unjustly enriched at S's expense, (ii) there is no legal justification for T's retention of the enrichment, and (iii) S has suffered a corresponding loss.
ugf_response: It is true that doing too little can cause big trouble because you cannot see when your own good ways lead to a bad end. We should try to use better ways for all people, not just use them for a short time.
